allergens: gluten (gram flour / besan may contain traces)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Prawn Koliwada is approximately 150 g, representing a standard snack or starter portion of 6–8 medium-sized battered and deep-fried prawns.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods used as the primary approved source for prawn and gram flour nutritional values. Prawn Koliwada as a composite dish is not listed individually; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawns and gram flour (besan) with adjustment for deep-frying oil absorption, consistent with the input enriched record. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) consulted as secondary cross-reference for prawn micronutrient profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for gram flour (besan) battered fried food
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Koliwada. GI of 40 is estimated as low based on: (1) gram flour (besan) has a published GI of approximately 28–39 (University of Sydney GI Database, chickpea flour-based foods); (2) high protein content from prawns (18.5 g/100g) further attenuates postprandial glucose response; (3) fat from deep-frying slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 10.2) / 100 = 4.1 (carbohydrate per 150g serving = 6.8 × 1.5 = 10.2g). This is a defensible analogous estimate; direct GI testing of Prawn Koliwada is not available in approved sources.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100g; ~630 mg per 150g serving) from salt in marinade and batter, combined with deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Prawn Koliwada due to its high sodium load (~630 mg per serving), which can elevate blood pressure; if consumed, portion should be restricted to no more than 75g and paired with low-sodium accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and high protein (18.5 g/100g) support glycemic control, but deep-frying adds significant fat and calories that may affect weight management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Koliwada has a low GI (~40) and high protein content that moderates postprandial glucose rise, making occasional consumption acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion control (≤150g) is essential given the caloric density from deep-frying (230 kcal/100g) and the carbohydrate contribution of the gram flour batter.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat (14.2 g/100g) and cholesterol (~145 mg/100g from prawns); however, prawns provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and selenium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Prawn Koliwada in moderation (≤100g occasionally) due to elevated total fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying and marinade; the omega-3 content from prawns offers some cardiovascular benefit but is partially offset by the pro-inflammatory effects of deep-frying in refined oil.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Primary ingredient is prawns (crustacean shellfish), a major allergen
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Prawn Koliwada must be strictly avoided by individuals with shellfish or crustacean allergies, as prawns are the primary ingredient and cross-contamination risk is high in traditional preparation settings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Koliwada is a popular deep-fried seafood snack originating from the Koli fishing communities (Koliwadas) of Mumbai and the broader Konkan coastal belt of Maharashtra, India. The dish is prepared by marinating fresh prawns in a spiced paste of ginger, garlic, red chili powder, turmeric, and carom seeds (ajwain), then coating them in a thick batter made from gram flour (besan) before deep-frying in hot oil until golden and crispy. The result is a fiery-red, crunchy-exterior snack with a juicy, well-seasoned prawn interior and a distinctive aroma from the ajwain. It is traditionally served with fried green chilies and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 230 kcal, 18.5g protein, 14.2g fat, and 6.8g carbohydrate, with a low glycemic index of approximately 40 due to the high protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption. The sodium content is high at 420 mg/100g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. Prawns contribute significant vitamin B12 (2.2 mcg/100g), selenium (35 mcg/100g), and phosphorus (210 mg/100g). The dish is not suitable for those with shellfish allergies. It is commonly served as a starter, evening snack, or component of a coastal seafood platter at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Prawn Koliwada as a composite prepared dish is not individually listed in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawns and gram flour with deep-frying oil absorption adjustment, consistent with the input enriched record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chickpea flour data. Confidence is medium because no single approved source provides an exact match for this dish.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for Prawn Koliwada as a deep-fried composite dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation; limited published data for this traditional deep-fried preparation. 2. Saturated fat (2.8g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.4g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.1g), trans_fat_g (0.1g), and cholesterol_mg (145mg) values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp (USDA FDC ID 175180) and gram flour, adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption; these are not directly reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish. 3. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for gram flour / chickpea flour-based foods and the protein-fat attenuation effect; no direct GI measurement for Prawn Koliwada exists in approved sources. 4. Portions in the input record contained three identical duplicate entries (150g each); deduplicated to a single 150g serving as the canonical default. 5. This is an Indian / Maharashtrian dish; multilingual naming rules require Hindi (satisfied: प्रॉन कोलीवाड़ा) and Marathi romanized form (Kolambi Koliwada). Singaporean multilingual rules (Chinese, Malay, Tamil) do not apply to this cuisine category. 6. No core macros are zero; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
